coredata.dc:title: Hierarchical attention-based framework for enhanced prediction and optimization of organic and inorganic material synthesis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Munsif, Muhammad
Hussain, Altaf
Khan, Zulfiqar Ahmad
Kim, Min Je
Baik, Sung Wook
coredata.dc:description:
Optimizing the synthesis of organic and inorganic materials, including molybdenum disulfide (MoS
2
), and estimating the photoluminescent quantum yield (PLQY) remains a complex and time-intensive challenge with significant applications in high-impact areas such as energy storage, light-emitting devices, and light-filtering materials. Traditional machine learning approaches like XGBoost and support vector machines (SVMs) have shown effectiveness in predicting material properties; however, they often require manual feature engineering and are limited in capturing intricate dependencies across experimental parameters. To address these limitations, this study proposes a unified hierarchical attention transformer network (HATNet) that leverages the multi-head-attention (MHA) mechanism to automatically learn complex interactions within feature spaces, providing a more flexible and powerful alternative for synthesis optimization. Our proposed framework is applied to two key tasks: MoS
2
growth status classification and carbon quantum dot (CQD) PLQY estimation. This framework captures high-order feature dependencies in small and large datasets for regression and classification through a shared attention-based encoder. The experimental results demonstrate that HATNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving higher predictive performance, with a 95% classification accuracy for MoS
2
synthesis and a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.003 on inorganic compositions and 0.0219 on organic compositions for carbon quantum yield estimation. These results illustrate HATNet’s adaptability and accuracy in synthesizing advanced materials, highlighting its versatility as a tool for guiding experimental synthesis across various materials in the field of materials science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Material informatics
Material synthesis
Deep learning
Molybdenum disulfide
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Discussion and comparison with state-of-the-art techniques
Material synthesis acceleration and reduction of trial-and-error experiments

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Nano & Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT, Republic of Korea . Project Name: Data HUB for Solid Electrolyte Materials Based on SyncroLab Data Cloud, Grant Number: RS-2024-00446825 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The abstract workflow of the framework.

The overall proposed framework consists of three stages: (1) data collection and preprocessing, where raw data are gathered, cleaned, and prepared for analysis; (2) the proposed learning network, where the model is designed and learns from the preprocessed data to identify patterns and make predictions; and (3) model evaluation and deployment, where the model’s performance is assessed and refined to ensure accuracy and reliability for real-world deployment.
Kendall correlation heatmap displaying monotonic associations among experimental variables and the PLQY.
Pearson correlation heatmap showing linear relationships among key experimental factors and PLQY outcomes.
Kendall’s tau correlation heatmap showing linear relationships among key experimental factors and MoS2 outcomes.
Pearson correlation heatmap showing linear relationships among key experimental factors and MoS2 outcomes.

Confusion matrix for MoS
2
conditions specification task of the proposed framework.

Comparing actual versus estimated inorganic material quantum yield values (%). Each point represents a data sample, with the predicted quantum yield plotted on the y -axis against the actual quantum yield on the x -axis. The red dashed line indicates the ideal 1:1 correspondence, suggesting model accuracy in capturing the quantum yield.
Density plot of actual and predicted quantum yield values (%). The red curve represents the actual data distribution, while the blue curve indicates the predicted distribution.

Probability density plot of actual and predicted organic composition-based quantum yield values (%). The blue curve represents the actual data distribution, while the orange curve indicates the predicted distribution.

Comparing actual versus estimated organic compound-based quantum yield values (%). Each point represents a data sample, with the predicted quantum yield plotted on the y -axis against the actual quantum yield on the x -axis. The red dashed line indicates the ideal 1:1 correspondence, suggesting model accuracy in capturing the quantum yield.
Comparative performance evaluation (regression) for inorganic CQD synthesis conditions.
Comparative performance evaluation (regression) for the internal ablation study of the proposed models.
Comparative performance evaluation (classification) for inorganic MoS2 synthesis conditions.
Internal ablation study of proposed model for inorganic MoS2 synthesis conditions.
Comparison of state-of-the-art regression methods for inorganic CQD synthesis conditions.
Comparison of state-of-the-art classification methods for inorganic MoS2 synthesis conditions.
Comparison of State-of-the-Art Regression Methods for Organic CQD Synthesis Conditions.

head.ce:title: Hierarchical attention-based framework for enhanced prediction and optimization of organic and inorganic material synthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sejong University, Seoul 143-747, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sejong University , Seoul 143-747, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Optimizing the synthesis of organic and inorganic materials, including molybdenum disulfide (MoS
2
), and estimating the photoluminescent quantum yield (PLQY) remains a complex and time-intensive challenge with significant applications in high-impact areas such as energy storage, light-emitting devices, and light-filtering materials. Traditional machine learning approaches like XGBoost and support vector machines (SVMs) have shown effectiveness in predicting material properties; however, they often require manual feature engineering and are limited in capturing intricate dependencies across experimental parameters. To address these limitations, this study proposes a unified hierarchical attention transformer network (HATNet) that leverages the multi-head-attention (MHA) mechanism to automatically learn complex interactions within feature spaces, providing a more flexible and powerful alternative for synthesis optimization. Our proposed framework is applied to two key tasks: MoS
2
growth status classification and carbon quantum dot (CQD) PLQY estimation. This framework captures high-order feature dependencies in small and large datasets for regression and classification through a shared attention-based encoder. The experimental results demonstrate that HATNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving higher predictive performance, with a 95% classification accuracy for MoS
2
synthesis and a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.003 on inorganic compositions and 0.0219 on organic compositions for carbon quantum yield estimation. These results illustrate HATNet’s adaptability and accuracy in synthesizing advanced materials, highlighting its versatility as a tool for guiding experimental synthesis across various materials in the field of materials science.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Material synthesis remains a significant challenge in the advancement of cutting-edge organic and inorganic materials. Complex synthesis processes often involve substantial uncertainties and are both costly and time-consuming [1–3]. For instance, two-dimensional (2D) materials have garnered considerable research attention in recent years due to their unique and remarkable properties, enabling applications in transistors [4], sensors [5], photo detectors [6], batteries [7], and flexible electronics [8]. Among various synthesis techniques, chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is regarded as one of the most promising methods for achieving the controllable and scalable production of these materials. However, the CVD process is influenced by numerous factors, such as the reaction temperature, chamber pressure, and carrier gas flow rate, which significantly increase its unpredictability [9]. Historically, the optimization of synthesis conditions has relied heavily on labor-intensive trial-and-error approaches, resulting in protracted development cycles [10,11]. The high number of experimental iterations, combined with the use of expensive precursors and substantial energy consumption, further escalates research and development costs [12,13].

This challenge is not exclusive to CVD; other multivariable synthesis techniques, such as hydrothermal methods for carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [13], chemical vapor transport (CVT) [14], atomic layer deposition (ALD) [15], and molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) [16], face similar issues [17]. Consequently, there is an urgent need for an effective artificial intelligence (AI)-based strategy to optimize and accelerate the synthesis of advanced organic and inorganic materials.

row.entry: Distance of Sulfur Outside the Furnace

ce:section.ce:para:
Recently, machine learning (ML) has demonstrated significant potential in accelerating materials development, such as for the discovery and design of materials for lithium-ion batteries [18], active materials for aqueous supercapacitors [19], solid-state materials synthesis [20], the design of stable organic light-emitting diode materials [21], and arch dam parameter optimization under discharge excitation [22]. In particular, ML models have been successfully applied to the discovery of new materials, such as metallic glasses [23], shape memory alloys [24], and inorganic–organic hybrid materials [25], as well as the prediction of critical material properties [26], including the electronic behavior of inorganic materials, the grain boundary energies of crystalline structures, phase transitions, and crystal structures [27]. However, material synthesis has a crucial step in transitioning from theoretical predictions to practical applications that remain comparatively underexplored [28]. With the advancement of high-throughput first-principles computations, the demand for efficient and controllable synthesis methods has grown, particularly to keep pace with the rapidly increasing number of predicted and screened materials. While a few pioneering studies have explored ML-guided synthesis, most have focused on investigating specific chemical reactions, such as for ML-driven catalyst design [29], for exploring catalytic reactions [30], and for using catalysis for predicting hydrogen evolution reaction activity [31]. In addition, [32] employs XGBoost-based ML models to optimize and accelerate the synthesis of advanced inorganic materials by predicting optimal synthesis conditions and minimizing trial-and-error efforts. The approach improves the success rate of CVD-grown MoS
2
and enhances the photoluminescent quantum yield of hydrothermal-grown CQDs, demonstrating the effectiveness of XGBoost in material development. Similarly, [33] develops ML models using gradient boosting regression trees (GBRTs) to predict the photophysical properties of organic fluorescent materials, specifically emission wavelengths and photoluminescent quantum yields (PLQYs). In [34], ML is used to predict the photophysical properties of organic dyes, with the quadratic support vector machine (QSVM) providing the best accuracy. Similarly, [35] uses hybrid ML models to predict the photophysical properties of organic materials, achieving a high accuracy and outperforming previous models, enabling the faster design of fluorescent materials. Most of the aforementioned methods fail to address the broader challenge of synthesizing materials for practical applications. Furthermore, existing ML approaches are primarily focused on guiding either inorganic or organic synthesis, with advanced materials still facing limitations in generalized predictive accuracy [36]. This underscores the need for an advanced deep learning (DL)-based approach for further development in this area.

Despite recent advances in materials synthesis using ML and DL methods, the current literature presents several limitations that our work aims to address. Many state-of-the-art models — such as traditional approaches (e.g., SVMs, XGBoost) and even some recent DL architectures — rely heavily on manual feature engineering. This reliance restricts their ability to automatically capture the complex, high-order interactions inherent in high-dimensional synthesis data. Moreover, while these models have shown reasonable performance in either inorganic or organic material domains, they generally do not offer a unified solution capable of handling multiple synthesis challenges concurrently. Specifically, several shortcomings can be identified in the current state-of-the-art methods:

ce:list-item.ce:para:
Existing ML-driven approaches, such as predictive modeling for understanding the growth mechanisms of MoS
2
material layers [38], the ML-assisted synthesis of CQDs [39], and the ML-based analysis of inorganic nanomaterials for cancer treatment [40], are primarily designed for inorganic materials. However, these methods often lack the adaptability to address diverse synthesis challenges within a unified framework. For instance, tasks such as the classification of MoS
2
structures and the estimation of the carbon quantum yield require distinct computational strategies. To overcome these limitations, it is crucial to develop comprehensive architectures capable of handling multiple synthesis-related problems within a single, integrative framework, thereby enhancing the generalizability and efficiency of ML-driven material discovery.

Current methods, such as those of [32,38,39], frequently rely on statistical or conventional ML-based methods like SVMs and XGBoost, which often fail to achieve a high accuracy. Furthermore, there exist some automatic ML model parameter optimization techniques, such as an improved KNN-based method (E-TKNN) to optimize squeeze casting parameters using past data, entropy-based weighting, and SVM-RFE for feature selection, achieving a higher accuracy and efficiency than traditional experimental approaches [41]. However, its reliance on historical data limits adaptability to new materials, and the method’s effectiveness depends on the diversity and quality of the existing dataset. In addition, [42] proposes a backpropagation (BP) neural network with an improved sparrow search algorithm (LCSSA) to intelligently optimize squeeze casting parameters, achieving better accuracy and robustness than genetic and swarm optimization algorithms. However, these automatic ML optimization methods still need improvement to achieve the most satisfactory and accurate performance. As a result, these methods still require trial-and-error experimentation during the synthesis of nanoinorganic materials. More robust and advanced accurate models need to be proposed to further reduce the time and effort required for material synthesis.

ce:section.ce:para:
To address the challenges outlined above, we propose the hierarchical attention transformer network (HATNet) framework, designed to overcome existing limitations in material synthesis. This robust and generalized framework accelerates the material synthesis process by minimizing trial and error, enabling the simultaneous handling of distinct tasks for both organic and inorganic materials, such as MoS
2
classification and carbon quantum yield regression. While these tasks share common synthesis conditions — such as the temperature, precursor concentration, and reaction time — they also involve unique factors; for example, MoS2 properties are influenced by the layer thickness, whereas the CQD yield depends on specific synthesis dynamics. By maintaining separate weights for each task, our approach optimizes feature extraction from shared variables while ensuring task-specific accuracy. This results in enhanced efficiency, a reduced computational load, and streamlined production, all without compromising performance. The key contributions of our proposed work are summarized as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Instead of focusing solely on property prediction or exploring underlying mechanisms, we propose a HATNet-based DL framework (abstract representation is given in Fig. 1) to accelerate the synthesis of both inorganic and organic CQDs and MoS2. Our framework accurately identifies optimal experimental conditions, minimizing the need for extensive physical trial-and-error experiments in the laboratory.

Unlike conventional ML methods, the proposed framework utilizes a deep HATNet architecture with cascaded encoders, designed to capture fine-grained relationships in CQD and MoS2 nanomaterial experimental conditions. Multiple feature-learning layers are incorporated to extract critical features, enabling more precise and reliable predictions.

The framework is evaluated on two distinct tasks, including CQD photoluminescent quantum yield estimation and MoS2 growth status classification with minimal modifications. Using real-world data, the proposed framework achieved a state-of-the-art performance by achieving a high predictive accuracy, with a 95% classification accuracy for MoS2 synthesis and a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.003 on inorganic compositions and 0.0219 on organic compositions for carbon quantum yield estimation. This demonstrates the framework’s versatility and ability to be extended to other materials with minimal adjustments or fine-tuning.

ce:section.ce:para: The remainder of the paper is organized as follows: Section 2 provides an in-depth discussion of the technical details of the proposed methodology. Section 3 presents the datasets and experimental results, including both qualitative and quantitative analysis. Finally, the conclusions are summarized in Section 4.

In this section, we introduce the methodology proposed for predicting MoS2 growth and the CQD quantum yield using HATNet. We start with data preprocessing, where key attributes are identified based on correlation analyses to prioritize influential experimental conditions. Following this, we provide a detailed overview of the internal workings of HATNet. The framework is depicted in Fig. 2, with the operational procedure outlined in Algorithm 1. Each step is described in the following subsections.

The dataset used in this study for inorganic materials was acquired through real laboratory experiments [32]. Both MoS2 and CQDs are considered under various experimental conditions, such as the temperature and other attributes, which are further elaborated in the results section. To prepare the data for training the proposed network, several preprocessing steps were applied, as shown in Fig. 2 (Step 1), including attribute refinement and selection. Although the raw data contain numerous attributes, for the growth of CQDs, six hydrothermal parameters were empirically identified as the most significant input features: the pH value (pH), reaction temperature (T), reaction time (t), mass of precursor A (M), ramp rate (Rr), and solution volume (V). The dataset for organic materials based on the PLQY presented by [33] is used; it contains 4386 instances and 11 attributes describing organic fluorescent materials. For this work, the preprocessing pipeline focused on transforming and preparing the data for the learning models. The attributes in the dataset include five solvatochromic parameters, three photophysical properties (maximum absorption wavelength, emission wavelength, and quantum yield), SMILES representations of materials, material-associated solvents in SMILES format, and reference information. To represent the molecular structures of organic fluorescent materials and their associated solvents, the SMILES strings were converted into Morgan fingerprints. These molecular fingerprints, available in the RDKit library [43], provide a computationally efficient representation by encoding molecules into binary vectors (0 s and 1 s), enabling easy comparison across molecular structures. Morgan fingerprints were selected due to their high general applicability and their proven effectiveness in high-throughput materials screening workflows [44]. Further key preprocessing steps included the removal of incomplete or erroneous records and the encoding of molecular information into machine-readable formats. The distributions of key photophysical properties, including the molecular weight, absorption wavelength, emission wavelength, and quantum yield, were analyzed. These properties exhibited ranges from 95–1676 g/mol for the molecular weight, 260–1026 nm for the absorption wavelength, 296–1045 nm for the emission wavelength, and 0–1 for the quantum yield, ensuring diverse data coverage for training predictive models, and these attributes’ correlations were considered for training and to evaluate the proposed framework. In addition, for identifying whether MoS2 is grown or not, the key attributes consist of the distance of sulfur outside the furnace (D), gas flow rate (Rf), ramp time (tr), reaction temperature (T), reaction time (t), addition of NaCl, and boat configuration (F/T), where “F” represents a flat boat and “T” a tilted boat. The boat refers to the container where precursors are placed during the CVD reaction. Furthermore, two standard methods were applied to ensure the relationships between input and output variables: linear (Pearson’s) and monotonic (Kendall’s) correlation coefficients. Pearson and Kendall correlation analyses are performed to capture different types of relationships between features. Pearson analysis is used to measure linear relationships, identifying direct proportional dependencies, while Kendall analysis is employed to capture non-linear relationships, offering robustness against outliers and providing a more comprehensive view of feature interdependencies. This dual approach ensures that both linear and non-linear correlations are considered during feature selection. Pearson’s correlation coefficient was computed using Eq. (1):
(1)
R
p
=
∑
(
x
i
−
x
̄
)
(
t
i
−
t
̄
)
∑
(
x
i
−
x
̄
)
2
∑
(
t
i
−
t
̄
)
2
where
R
p
is Pearson’s correlation coefficient,
x
i
and
t
i
represent the input variables and their respective values, and
x
̄
and
t
̄
are their mean values. Kendall’s tau coefficient was calculated using Eq. (2):
(2)
τ
r
=
C
K
−
D
K
C
K
+
D
K
where
τ
r
is Kendall’s tau coefficient, and
C
K
and
D
K
denote the number of concordant and discordant pairs, respectively, between the ranked input variables. Both Pearson’s and Kendall’s correlation coefficients yield values in the range
[ − 1 , 1 ]
. A value of 1 indicates a strong positive correlation,
− 1
indicates a strong negative correlation, and 0 signifies no relationship. Values between 1 and
− 1
indicate varying degrees of correlation between the input variables and the output variable. To refine the input attribute selection and ensure proper dependency on the selected attributes, we averaged both correlation coefficients and selected features with coefficients in the ranges
[ 0 . 02 , 1 ]
for positive correlations and
[ − 1 , − 0 . 02 ]
for negative correlations. The ranges
[ 0 . 02 , 1 ]
for positive correlations and [−1, −0.02] for negative correlations were selected to focus on moderate to strong relationships. The positive range filters out weak correlations (below 0.02) that might introduce noise, while the negative range excludes correlations closer to zero, ensuring that only significant negative relationships are considered, improving model stability and reducing redundancy. The correlation of selected attributes with the target variables, as well as among the attributes for both MoS2 and CQDs, is illustrated in Figs. 3–6. For CQDs, Figs. 3 and 4 demonstrate that all selected attributes exhibit either positive or negative correlations. Similarly, Figs. 5 and 6 confirm suitable correlations for MoS2, indicating that these attributes are appropriate for further processing.

The selected attributes were then processed to handle missing values. As certain measurements may occasionally record zero values, these were replaced using a moving average technique. Additionally, to address significant differences in recorded values due to uncertain experimental conditions, standardization was applied. We utilized the standard scalar method, as given in Eq. (3):
(3)
z =
t − m
σ

where z represents the standardized value of the input attribute t , and m and σ are the mean and standard deviation of the attribute, respectively. Finally, to facilitate the training and evaluation of the model, the preprocessed data were split into training, testing, and validation sets. The detailed procedures for training and evaluation are discussed in the results section.

In the field of material property prediction, traditional ML models such as SVMs [45] and XGBoost [46] have been widely adopted for both classification and regression tasks. These conventional methods, while effective in certain scenarios, rely heavily on manual feature engineering and often struggle to capture complex, non-linear dependencies in high-dimensional data. For example, XGBoost, a tree-based boosting model, has been shown to perform well in many tabular data scenarios and has achieved notable success in material classification and regression tasks [32]. However, it lacks the ability to model sequential or highly interconnected dependencies within features without extensive feature engineering. Similarly, an SVM with non-linear kernels has been applied to material classification and regression tasks but can be computationally demanding for large datasets and often requires substantial tuning to achieve optimal results [34]. In light of these limitations, we propose a unified transformer-based architecture that leverages the power of self-attention mechanisms for both MoS
2
classification and CQD regression tasks. The proposed architecture provides a more adaptive and capable alternative compared to traditional models by automatically learning complex interactions between features, which is particularly advantageous for high-dimensional material data. The input variables used by the HATNet architecture for MoS2 classification include the distance of sulfur outside the furnace (D), gas flow rate (Rf), ramp time (tr), reaction temperature (T), reaction time (t), addition of NaCl (Yes/No), and boat configuration (flat or tilted). For CQD regression, the input variables consist of the pH value of the solution, reaction temperature (T), reaction time (t), mass of precursor A (M), ramp rate (Rr), and solution volume (V). These features have complex interactions with each other, which is particularly advantageous in allowing HATNet to learn high-dimensional dependencies.

The proposed model is a generalized attention-based architecture that is flexible enough to handle both classification for MoS
2
and regression for CQD quantum yield prediction. The architecture of the proposed HATNet, illustrated in Fig. 2 (Step 2), leverages a transformer-based framework to process material experiment features. Initially, raw features undergo embedding and are passed through multiple transformer encoders, which incorporate multi-head attention (MHA) mechanisms and layer normalization (LN) to enhance stability and training efficiency. The fully connected layers (FC1, FC2, FC3) further refine the learned representations, ensuring robust feature extraction and transformation. By leveraging the attention mechanism, the model captures intricate dependencies among input features without requiring manual feature selection or extensive preprocessing. To ensure uniform scaling and promote stability during training, input data, whether for classification or regression, are first normalized using MinMaxScaler (Algorithm 1), mapping feature values to between 0 and 1. This is followed by standardization, ensuring a mean of zero and unit variance. At the core of HATNet lies the transformer encoder, designed to model complex relationships between features. Each encoder consists of multiple MHA layers, enabling features to interact dynamically. For each input vector, the encoder generates query (Q), key (K), and value (V) matrices, and the self-attention score is computed as follows:
(4)
Attention
( Q , K , V )
= softmax
Q
K
⊤
d
k
V
where
d
k
represents the dimensionality of the key vectors. Eq. (4) enables each feature in the input to weight the importance of other features, allowing the model to learn high-order interactions across the feature set. This equipped the proposed framework with capabilities that traditional models like XGBoost and SVMs lack. Our encoder also incorporates MHA, where multiple attention heads allow the model to focus on different aspects of the data simultaneously. After the attention mechanism, the refined embeddings are processed independently through a feed-forward network to capture non-linear interactions, formulated as follows:
(5)
FFN
( x )
= max
( 0 , x
W
1
+
b
1
)
W
2
+
b
2
where
W
1
,
W
2
,
b
1
, and
b
2
are learnable parameters. Layer normalization and dropout are applied after each self-attention and feed-forward layer, enhancing gradient flow, stabilizing training, and reducing overfitting. After HATNet’s encoder processes the feature relationships, the encoded output is passed through fully connected layers to perform the final classification or regression tasks. The fully connected layers consist of three intermediate layers, each followed by ReLU activation, dropout, and LN to enhance non-linearity and reduce overfitting. The architecture then diverges in its output configuration based on the task type.

For the MoS2 classification task, the final output layer consists of a single unit with a sigmoid activation function:
(6)
y
ˆ
= σ
(
W
3
x +
b
3
)
where σ represents the sigmoid function,
W
3
and
b
3
are learnable weights and biases, and the output
y
ˆ
represents the probability of the sample belonging to a particular class. The model is trained using the binary cross-entropy (BCE) loss:
(7)
BCE
( y ,
y
ˆ
)
= −
y log
(
y
ˆ
)
+
( 1 − y )
log
( 1 −
y
ˆ
)

which is suitable for binary classification tasks, minimizing the error between predicted probabilities and actual labels. For the CQD regression task, the output layer is configured as a single unit without an activation function, suitable for predicting continuous values. The output in this case represents the predicted quantum yield of the CQDs:
(8)
y
ˆ
=
W
3
x +
b
3

This continuous output layer enables the model to perform regression, optimized using the MSE loss:
(9)
MSE
( y ,
y
ˆ
)
=
1
N
∑
i = 1
N
(
y
i
−
y
ˆ
i
)
2
where
y
i
is the actual quantum yield,
y
ˆ
i
is the predicted yield, and N represents the number of samples. The MSE loss penalizes larger errors more significantly, encouraging precise predictions. The proposed HATNet offers several advantages over traditional methods. Unlike these conventional models, which rely on manual feature engineering, HATNet accurately learns complex feature interactions via MHA. This is especially beneficial for high-dimensional datasets, which are common in material science. Moreover, the proposed framework can be adapted to both classification and regression tasks by simply modifying the output layer and loss function, which adds to its versatility.

In this section, we present the experimental results obtained using the proposed HATNet framework for predicting MoS2 growth and the PLQY. First, we describe the experimental setup and datasets employed in our evaluation. Subsequently, we compare the predictive performance of HATNet against conventional modeling approaches, demonstrating that HATNet achieves superior accuracy and reduced error metrics. Finally, we discuss the limitations of the current framework and specify future directions for enhancing model generalization.

All experiments were conducted on a Windows-based PC, equipped with an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU, 48 GB of RAM, and an AMD Ryzen 9 3900X 12-core processor running at 3.79 GHz. The framework was developed using Python 3.8, along with PyTorch version 1.12.1. To optimize the performance of key parameters within the proposed HATNet framework, such as the number of attention heads, number of encoder layers, learning rate, and number of epochs, a grid search strategy is employed. This approach systematically explored a wide range of parameter combinations, including the learning rate, weight decay, and momentum, to determine the most effective configurations. Both the PLQY and MoS
2
models were trained end-to-end using the ADAM optimizer, with fixed hyperparameters. These include a momentum term (
M = 0 . 9
) and a learning rate (
LR = 0 . 001
). For both tasks, the models were trained over 1000 epochs with a batch size of 32. The evaluation metrics varied depending on the task. For the MoS2 task, the accuracy ( A ), specificity (
S
p
), and sensitivity (
S
e
) were computed using Eqs. (12)–(14), while for the PLQY estimation task, the MSE and the coefficient of determination (
R
2
) were obtained using Eqs. (10) and (11):
(10)
MSE =
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
(
y
i
−
y
ˆ
i
)
2
(11)
R
2
= 1 −
∑
i = 1
n
(
y
i
−
y
ˆ
i
)
2
∑
i = 1
n
(
y
i
−
y
̄
)
2
(12)
A =
T P + T N
T P + T N + F P + F N
(13)
S
p
=
T N
T N + F P
(14)
S
e
=
T P
T P + F N
In these equations,
y
i
represents the actual observed values, while
y
ˆ
i
represents the predicted values.
y
̄
is the mean of the actual observed values, and n is the total number of observations. For classification metrics,
T P
(true positives) refers to the number of correctly predicted positive cases, and
T N
(true negatives) refers to the number of correctly predicted negative cases.
F P
(false positives) represents the number of incorrectly predicted positive cases, while
F N
(false negatives) represents the number of incorrectly predicted negative cases. A detailed analysis of both the quantitative and qualitative results is provided in the subsequent subsections.

All experiments were conducted under conditions optimized for CQD and MoS
2
synthesis, based on data reported in [32,33]. For CQD yield estimation using a small inorganic dataset [32], the most significant hydrothermal parameters, including the pH, temperature, reaction time, precursor mass, ramp rate, and solution volume, were identified for the optimization process. A total of 467 experimental records were extracted from archived laboratory notebooks [32], each documenting various growth parameters and their corresponding PLQY values, which ranged from 0 to 1. To evaluate the performance on a relatively large organic dyes dataset, a dataset comprising over 3000 instances and 11 attributes for PLQY estimation after preprocessing, discussed in Subsection 2.1, was sourced from [33]. The attributes included the absorption, emission, quantum yield, and SMILES representations of the dyes, as well as associated references (DOIs). Solvent-related attributes were captured using five solvatochromic parameters. Of the 11 attributes, nine were used as input features, while the quantum yield was designated as the outcome variable. Both of the datasets are divided into training (90%) and testing (10%) sets to train and validate the performance of the proposed framework, respectively. Achieving a high PLQY is critical for quantum dot applications. Therefore, this study specifically focused on optimizing conditions to enhance the PLQY.

For the CVD-grown MoS
2
dataset, 300 experimental data points were collected from archived laboratory records [32]. The detailed synthesis process feature selection procedure for MoS
2
is described in the method section. Of these 300 experiments, successful MoS
2
growth was achieved in 183 cases (61%), while the remaining 117 experiments resulted in negative outcomes (39%). A binary classification problem was formulated to distinguish between successful and unsuccessful MoS
2
growth. Specifically, for MoS
2
growth classification, a threshold of a lateral size of
1
μ m
was used to differentiate successful growth (“Can grow”) from unsuccessful growth (“Cannot grow”). This threshold is based on the limitations of optical microscopy, where it becomes challenging to differentiate between actual samples and nucleation sites when their size is below
1
μ m
. Furthermore, MoS
2
samples smaller than
1
μ m
have limited practical utility for most applications.

We conducted a comprehensive ablation study to develop a state-of-the-art model. Our evaluation included a variety of methods, ranging from traditional learning classifiers and regressors to tree-based approaches like the random forest (RF) and decision tree (DT), as well as gradient-based techniques such as gradient boosting (GB) and LightGBM. We also explored optimization methods such as the E-TKNN [41], LCSSA [42], and DL methods, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and attention-based models utilizing self-attention and MHA mechanisms. Furthermore, we examined internal variations of the proposed methods, specifically testing different attention mechanisms across both regression and classification tasks. Table 1 presents the regression performance of various models on inorganic CQD yield synthesis conditions. The MSE and
R
2
values are used as performance metrics. The proposed HATNet model achieves the best performance, with the lowest MSE of 0.0030 and the highest
R
2
of 0.9075, indicating a significant improvement in prediction accuracy ( A ) compared to traditional methods. Table 3 summarizes the classification performance of several models for MoS
2
synthesis conditions, measured by A ,
S
e
, and
S
p
. HATNet outperforms other methods, achieving the highest A of 95.00%,
S
e
of 0.9412, and
S
p
of 0.9615. This model demonstrates superior classification capabilities due to its internal use of hierarchical attention-based encoders, making it the best choice for predicting MoS
2
synthesis outcomes. To further refine our model selection and optimization, we performed an internal ablation study, as summarized in Tables 2 and 4. Table 2 presents the comparative performance evaluation for regression tasks. We varied the encoder dimension and the number of layers, assessing their impact on the MSE and the coefficient of determination (
R
2
). Notably, increasing the encoder dimension and the number of layers contributed to a lower MSE and a higher
R
2
, with the best performance achieved at an encoder dimension of 122 and 8 layers (Ex.No 9: MSE = 0.0030,
R
2
= 0.9075). This indicates that a larger encoder dimension and deeper architectures improve the model’s ability to capture complex data patterns, making it highly effective for regression tasks.

Table 4 presents the classification performance of our proposed model in the context of inorganic MoS
2
synthesis conditions. The model was evaluated using accuracy ( A ), sensitivity (
S
e
), and specificity (
S
p
) across different architectural configurations. As observed, increasing the encoder dimension and layers improved the classification performance, with the highest accuracy of 95.00% recorded for an encoder dimension of 122 and 8 layers (Ex.No 9). Moreover, the sensitivity and specificity metrics show consistent improvements, particularly in later experiments, indicating a well-balanced model that minimizes false positives and false negatives. These findings confirm that increasing the model’s complexity enhances both regression and classification performance. The results further reinforce the significance of attention-based mechanisms and multi-layer architectures in capturing intricate dependencies within the data. Overall, our ablation study provides strong evidence that fine-tuning encoder dimensions and layers plays a crucial role in optimizing the model performance for diverse tasks, from regression to classification.

The proposed HATNet model’s predictive performance on the test dataset was analyzed qualitatively from various perspectives using various visualizations. These include a confusion matrix (Fig. 7), a prediction density plot (Figs. 9 and 10), and a learned scatter plot (Figs. 8 and 11), each offering unique insights into the model’s strengths. The confusion matrix shown in Fig. 7 provides a detailed view of HATNet’s classification accuracy, showcasing its ability to distinguish between the MoS
2
-grown and not-grown classification tasks. The matrix reveals 25 grown and 32 not-grown cases, with only three misclassifications: one false positive and two false negatives. This balance of high true positive and true negative counts relative to the low misclassification rate indicates that HATNet is highly effective for MoS
2
growth, demonstrating robust classification for MoS
2
conditions. The low number of false positives and false negatives highlights the model’s precision and reliability in minimizing both types of errors, suggesting a well-calibrated model with strong decision boundaries. The density plot shown in Fig. 9 for the inorganic PLQY estimation of HATNet provides a comparative analysis of the distribution of actual versus predicted quantum yield values, visualized as overlapping curves. The “Actual” distribution, represented by a red curve, closely overlaps with the “Predicted” distribution, shown in light blue. This visual alignment suggests that HATNet captures the general distribution of quantum yield values effectively, with only minor discrepancies. Notable slight deviations appear around specific peaks, but overall, the density plot suggests that HATNet maintains strong fidelity to the actual data distribution. Similarly, the prediction scatter plot shown in Fig. 8 of the actual versus predicted quantum yield estimation provides a direct assessment of the correlation between individual predictions and true values. Data points are tightly clustered around the ideal diagonal line, represented by a red dashed line, which signifies perfect prediction. The close alignment of points along this line indicates a high degree of predictive accuracy, with only minimal dispersion, implying that HATNet consistently produces accurate predictions across a range of quantum yield estimations. Minor deviations from the diagonal line are observed, but these are relatively small and do not significantly detract from the overall performance, underscoring the model’s ability to reliably indicate suitable conditions in a real materials synthesis environment. This high correlation reflects HATNet’s strength in capturing both linear and non-linear relationships in the inorganic data, a desirable quality in regression tasks where precise prediction is crucial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion and comparison with state-of-the-art techniques
ce:section.ce:para:
To better evaluate the proposed framework, we conducted a comparison with state-of-the-art frameworks using the same datasets used in existing studies for organic and inorganic materials. Table 5 presents a comparative analysis of various leading regression models applied to inorganic PLQYs. The proposed HATNet significantly outperforms the other methods, achieving the lowest MSE of 0.0030 and the highest
R
2
value of 0.9075. This demonstrates the superior performance of the proposed model in predicting CQD synthesis outcomes compared to previous techniques like GPR, SVR, and XGBoost. Table 6 compares the classification performances of different state-of-the-art models for MoS
2
synthesis conditions. The proposed HATNet demonstrates a superior performance, with the highest A (95.00%),
S
e
(0.9412), and
S
p
(0.9615). These results indicate that the proposed model demonstrates a superior generalization and classification performance ( A ) compared to existing models such as XGBoost, SVMs, and naive Bayes. This enhanced performance is attributed to the use of hierarchical attention-based encoders in the proposed framework, which enable the extraction of more robust and fine-grained features, ultimately leading to more accurate predictions.

To benchmark the proposed model’s performance on organic materials, Table 7 presents a comparison with state-of-the-art regression methods for organic fluorescent materials, including CSVM [34], LGBMR [33], and a hybrid XGBR+LGBR approach [35]. The comparison is based on the two key evaluation metrics used for the other datasets: the MSE and
R
2
. Our model, HATNet, achieves the lowest MSE of 0.0219 and the highest
R
2
of 0.7537, outperforming the state-of-the-art XGBR+LGBR hybrid model, which achieved an MSE of 0.0228 and an
R
2
of 0.7401. Compared to LGBMR and CSVM, HATNet demonstrates a substantial improvement, indicating its superior ability to capture the complex relationships between synthesis parameters and PLQY outcomes. These results collectively demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of HATNet for organic-based PLQY prediction. The qualitative results discussed in Section 3.4 validate the model’s predictive performance and ability to generalize across the dataset, while the comparison with state-of-the-art methods highlights its superior performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material synthesis acceleration and reduction of trial-and-error experiments
ce:section.ce:para: Previous frameworks such as those in [31,33,35] have significantly accelerated material synthesis by reducing the number of trial-and-error experiments. For example, ML-guided optimization methods such as the PAM by Tang et al. [32] previously demonstrated a 75.37% reduction in experimental trials, explicitly decreasing the number of trials required from 467 (traditional empirical methods) down to 115 using their adaptive approach. Although effective, these methods still exhibited limitations in predictive accuracy, necessitating further experimental validation and refinement. With the introduction of HATNet, the efficiency of material synthesis has been substantially enhanced due to its superior predictive capabilities. For MoS2 synthesis, HATNet achieves an accuracy of 95.00%, a notable improvement from XGBoost’s previously reported accuracy of 89.86% on identical datasets. This accuracy enhancement corresponds to an additional estimated 50.7% reduction in experimental trials beyond PAM’s benchmark, resulting in an overall experimental trial reduction of approximately 87.8%, from the original 467 trials down to approximately 57, followed the [32] calculation method. Moreover, HATNet demonstrates enhanced sensitivity (0.9412 compared to 0.9284) and significantly improved specificity (0.9615 compared to 0.8524), resulting in fewer unsuccessful synthesis experiments and thus reducing both time and precursor waste. Similarly, for PLQY optimization in CQDs, HATNet increases the R2 score from 0.8402 to 0.9075 and concurrently decreases the MSE from 0.0036 to 0.0030. These improvements allow HATNet to pinpoint optimal synthesis conditions more precisely, effectively reducing unnecessary trials beyond what previous ML methods have achieved. A key distinguishing feature of HATNet, compared to traditional ML models, lies in its ability to capture complex, non-linear dependencies between synthesis parameters, resulting in superior experimental recommendations. For MoS2, critical synthesis factors such as the gas flow rate, reaction temperature, and reaction time are more precisely fine-tuned, leading directly to a higher experimental success rate. Similarly, for CQDs, important parameters including pH, reaction temperature, and precursor concentrations are optimized with greater accuracy, yielding enhanced PLQYs with fewer experimental iterations. Due to these advancements in predictive accuracy, HATNet achieves a reduction in the number of necessary trial-and-error experiments even greater than the previously reported 75.37% benchmark from PAM, effectively translating into an estimated total trial reduction of approximately 87.8%. This superior performance directly results in more efficient material discovery, significantly shorter development cycles, and lower overall research costs.

While HATNet demonstrates significant advantages over traditional ML models in accurately predicting synthesis outcomes for MoS2 and CQDs, several areas for further investigation and development remain. First, the predictive performance of HATNet relies heavily on the quality, diversity, and quantity of the training data. Given the challenges associated with obtaining extensive, high-quality datasets in materials science, future efforts will explore few-shot learning and transfer learning approaches. These methods can enable robust predictive performance even in scenarios where experimental data is limited, thus improving the generalizability of HATNet across diverse materials and synthesis conditions. Additionally, although the transformer-based architecture effectively captures complex dependencies between synthesis parameters, interpreting these predictions in terms of causal physical or chemical insights remains challenging. To address this limitation, future research will investigate hybrid modeling strategies that integrate domain-specific scientific principles, such as thermodynamics or kinetics, directly within the deep learning framework. This integration will facilitate not only improved prediction accuracy but also provide a more interpretable and scientifically-grounded basis for the model’s recommendations. Another critical area for future exploration is the optimization of computational efficiency, as transformer-based architectures, including HATNet, are typically resource-intensive. To make the model more accessible and practically deployable, research will focus on investigating model compression techniques, such as pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation, aiming to reduce computational requirements without compromising predictive performance. Finally, future studies will systematically validate and further refine the adaptability and universality of our proposed framework by extending its application to additional material systems, synthesis methods, and property prediction tasks. Specifically, will explore classification and regression tasks within individual materials and across broader experimental conditions. Through such comprehensive validations, we aim to robustly establish HATNet’s versatility as a broadly applicable tool in material informatics.

In this study, we introduced a HATNet-based framework designed to streamline the synthesis of MoS
2
and CQDs, addressing the limitations of conventional ML approaches. Traditional models, including XGBoost and SVMs, often struggle with high-dimensional material data due to their reliance on manual feature engineering and limited capacity for capturing complex feature interactions. The proposed HATNet leverages multi-head self-attention to accurately learn high-order dependencies, enabling the accurate prediction of optimal synthesis conditions with minimal preprocessing. Our model demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across both classification and regression tasks, achieving a 95% accuracy for MoS
2
growth classification and an MSE of 0.003 on inorganic compositions and 0.0219 on organic compositions for CQD quantum yield estimation. These results underscore HATNet’s ability to generalize effectively across different material synthesis scenarios. The adaptability of the proposed architecture makes it a valuable asset for material informatics, capable of accelerating the synthesis process, reducing experimental iterations, and minimizing the costs associated with advanced inorganic materials development.

Muhammad Munsif: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Altaf Hussain: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Conceptualization. Zulfiqar Ahmad Khan: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Min Je Kim: Validation, Resources, Formal analysis. Sung Wook Baik: Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Nano & Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT, Republic of Korea . Project Name: Data HUB for Solid Electrolyte Materials Based on SyncroLab Data Cloud, Grant Number: RS-2024-00446825.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Process parameters design of squeeze casting through an improved KNN algorithm and existing data
